revision.text:
[[File:Mount Hiei from Kyoto - IMG 5701.JPG|thumb|right|300px|Mount Hiei]] '''Sannō Gongen''' (山王権現) is a {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} deity that emerged from the fusion of {{ill|mountain worship|ja|山岳信仰}} on Mount Hiei ({{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}), {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}, and {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} Buddhism. It serves as the {{ill|chinjugami|ja|鎮守神|lt=guardian deity}} of the Tendai school. It was also referred to as '''Hiyoshi Gongen''' or '''Hiyoshi Sannō Gongen'''. == History == Sannō Gongen is the {{ill|chinjugami|ja|鎮守神|lt=guardian deity}} of {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, formed through the fusion of {{ill|mountain worship|ja|山岳信仰}}, {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}, and {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} on Mount Hiei ({{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}). It is also sometimes used to refer to the {{ill|saijin|ja|祭神|lt=deities enshrined}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}. Sannō Gongen, as the deity of Mount Hiei, is also affectionately called "Hiyossan" (Hiyoshi-san) and is enshrined in all [[:Category:Hiyoshi Shrines|Hiyoshi Shrines]] nationwide, with {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}} serving as the head shrine[http://www.rekihaku.otsu.shiga.jp/news/1104.html "Special Exhibition on Kami and Buddhas in Ōmi" (Ōtsu City Museum of History, September 3, 2011)]. The term "Hiyoshi Sannō" refers to the blended faith that regarded Mount Hiei as a "sacred mountain," integrating the belief systems of both {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}} and {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}. At {{ill|Guoqing Temple|ja|国清寺}} on {{ill|Mount Tiantai|ja|天台山}}, where {{ill|Saichō|ja|最澄}} (Dengyō Daishi), the founder of Japanese Tendai Buddhism, studied, the guardian deity was '''Jizhu Sannō Yuanbi Zhenjun''' (地主山王元弼真君), a deified prince of {{ill|King Ling of Zhou|ja|霊王_(周)}} in {{ill|Daoism|ja|道教}}. When Saichō returned to Japan, he enshrined Sannō Gongen as the guardian deity of {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, modeling it after Guoqing Temple. The peak of {{ill|Otowa Mountain|ja|音羽山_(滋賀県・京都府)}}, known as Ushioyama in ancient times, was revered as a sacred place where households offered their first harvest to the gods[http://kanko.city.kyoto.lg.jp/detail.php?InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000415 Kyoto Tourism Navi - Hōgon-ji (Ushio Kannon, Ushioyama)]. This belief laid the foundation for the mountain worship of Hieyama (Mount Hiei). Additionally, the *Kojiki* mentions that [[Oyamakui no Kami]], also called Yamasue-no-Ōnushi-no-Kami, dwells on Mount Hiei (日枝山) and at {{ill|Matsuo Taisha|ja|松尾大社}} in {{ill|Kadono District|ja|葛野郡}}. Furthermore, from {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社}}, where {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主}} is worshipped as the *nigi-mitama* (gentle spirit) of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴神}}, the deity was enshrined at Mount Hiei. This process established {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}, making it one of the most syncretic shrines, serving as the guardian deity of the Tendai school and its temple complex[https://www.asahi.com/travel/hikyou/TKY201103270195.html "Japan's Tradition: Shinbutsu-shūgō (2)" - Asahi Shimbun Digital, March 28, 2011]. {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}} and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}} maintained a close relationship, with Enryaku-ji holding authority over appointments within Hiyoshi Taisha from the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} onward. As Tendai Buddhism spread throughout Japan, temples dedicated to Sannō Gongen, called '''Sannō-sha''', were established across the country. In addition to Sannō Gongen, Tendai Buddhism also enshrined {{ill|Hachioji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen}} at Mount Hiei and assigned {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹}} Buddhas to the 21 Sannō Shrines. At the beginning of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine priests of Hiyoshi Taisha attempted to abolish Shinbutsu-shūgō and establish a form of exclusive Shinto known as {{ill|Yoshida Shintō|ja|唯一神道|Yoshida Shintō=Yoshida Shintō|lt=Ikkō Shintō}}. This led them to burn Buddhist images and engage in an early attempt at {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}}, the movement to eliminate Buddhism. However, Enryaku-ji appealed to the shogunate, resulting in a court ruling against Hiyoshi Taisha. The shrine priests were exiled, and the shrine was placed under complete control of Enryaku-ji. During this period, Enryaku-ji established a rule that "any actions beyond festivals and cleaning must receive permission from Enryaku-ji". From that point on, Hiyoshi Taisha was also financially dependent on Enryaku-ji. == Separation of Kami and Buddhas / Haibutsu Kishaku == During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the government implemented {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離}}, leading to the abolition of Sannō Gongen, the guardian deity of Tendai Buddhism. Hiyoshi Taisha was one of the first sites to undergo the {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離令}} in {{ill|Keiō|ja|慶応}} 4 (1868). Hiyoshi Sannō Shrine was forcibly separated from Enryaku-jiBecause the Shinto faction engaged in aggressive destruction of Buddhist-related objects, the new government issued a directive on April 10, Keiō 4, to implement Shinbutsu Bunri more peacefully. and reorganized into {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}. When the shrine attempted to take custody of its buildings' keys from Enryaku-ji, they were refused. In response, the shrine administrator, {{ill|Kiju Shigekuni|ja|樹下茂国}}, along with the Shinto guards and 100 hired farmers, stormed the shrine and destroyed 124 Buddhist statues, artifacts, and scriptures, while confiscating 48 bronze objects, including temple gongs. The seized metal was repurposed for cannon and currency production. This incident set a precedent for Haibutsu Kishaku across Japan. Today, many remaining {{ill|Sannō-sha|ja|山王社}} shrines have become {{ill|Hie Shrines|ja|日枝神社}} or {{ill|Hiyoshi Shrines|ja|日吉神社}}, enshrining {{ill|Ōyamakui-no-Kami|ja|大山咋神}} as their principal deity. == Hie Sannō Mandala (日吉山王曼荼羅図) == '''Hie Sannō Mandala''' (日吉山王曼荼羅図) is a mandala depicting the Buddhas and deities enshrined at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}, composed according to the doctrines of {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} Buddhism. Based on its format, it can be classified as a {{ill|Miya Mandala|ja|宮曼荼羅}}, {{ill|Honjibutsu Mandala|ja|本地仏曼荼羅}}, or {{ill|Suijaku Mandala|ja|垂迹神曼荼羅}}. === Miya Mandala (宮曼荼羅図) === [[File:Sanno Miya Mandala.jpg|thumb|right|150px|Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala (Muromachi period)
The upper section displays the twenty-one shrine deities, their honjibutsu (Buddhist counterparts), and their {{ill|seed syllables|ja|種子 (密教)}}. The lower section provides a bird's-eye view of the twenty-one shrines, depicting two shrines at the summit of {{ill|Mount Hachioji|ja|八王子山}} and the shrine complex at the foot of the mountain.]] The Miya Mandala is a depiction of shrine landscapes from an aerial perspective. These mandalas were created so that individuals unable to visit the shrine in person could worship from home as a substitute for pilgrimage. Examples designated as Important Cultural Properties include "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala" (held by the {{ill|Yamato Bunkakan|ja|大和文華館}}, Nara City) and "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala" (held by {{ill|Hyakusaiji Temple|ja|百済寺}}, Higashiomi City), as well as "Silk-colored Sannō Hōtō Mandala" (held by the [[:en:Ōtani University|Ōtani University Museum]], Kyoto City). === Honjibutsu Mandala (本地仏曼荼羅図) === The Honjibutsu Mandala illustrates the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}} in their original Buddhist forms, depicting them seated as regular Buddhist statues within shrine buildings. === Suijaku Mandala (垂迹神曼荼羅図) === Contrasting with the Honjibutsu Mandala, the Suijaku Mandala depicts the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}} in their Shinto god forms. It portrays a scene where the deities of Mount Hiei gather in a single shrine. Examples designated as Important Cultural Properties include the "Hie Sannō Suijaku Mandala" (held by {{ill|Saikyōji Temple|ja|西教寺}}, Otsu City), as well as "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Suijaku Mandala" (held by {{ill|Sannō Lecture|ja|山王講}}, Kurano-tsuji Tomokura) and "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Jūzenji Mandala" (held by {{ill|Shinnyo-en|ja|真如苑}}, Tokyo). == Honjibutsu of the 21 Sannō Shrines (山王21社の本地仏) == {| class="wikitable" ! !! Former Name !! Honjibutsu !! Location |- |rowspan=7|Upper Seven Shrines
({{ill|Sannō Seven Shrines|ja|山王七社}}) |Dai-gu (Great Hiei)||{{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来}}|| |- |Ninomiya (Small Hiei)||{{ill|Medicine Buddha|ja|薬師如来}}|| |- |Shōshinji||{{ill|Amitabha In addition to Sannō Gongen, Tendai Buddhism also enshrined {{ill|Hachioji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen|12=draft}} at Mount Hiei and assigned {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹|12=draft}} Buddhas to the 21 Sannō Shrines. At the beginning of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, the shrine priests of Hiyoshi Taisha attempted to abolish Shinbutsu-shūgō and establish a form of exclusive Shinto known as {{ill|Yoshida Shintō|ja|唯一神道|Yoshida Shintō=Yoshida Shintō|lt=Ikkō Shintō|12=draft}}. This led them to burn Buddhist images and engage in an early attempt at {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|12=draft}}, the movement to eliminate Buddhism. However, Enryaku-ji appealed to the shogunate, resulting in a court ruling against Hiyoshi Taisha. The shrine priests were exiled, and the shrine was placed under complete control of Enryaku-ji. During this period, Enryaku-ji established a rule that "any actions beyond festivals and cleaning must receive permission from Enryaku-ji". From that point on, Hiyoshi Taisha was also financially dependent on Enryaku-ji. == Separation of Kami and Buddhas / Haibutsu Kishaku == During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, the government implemented {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}}, leading to the abolition of Sannō Gongen, the guardian deity of Tendai Buddhism. Hiyoshi Taisha was one of the first sites to undergo the {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離令|12=draft}} in {{ill|Keiō|ja|慶応|12=draft}} 4 (1868). Hiyoshi Sannō Shrine was forcibly separated from Enryaku-jiBecause the Shinto faction engaged in aggressive destruction of Buddhist-related objects, the new government issued a directive on April 10, Keiō 4, to implement Shinbutsu Bunri more peacefully. and reorganized into {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft}}. When the shrine attempted to take custody of its buildings' keys from Enryaku-ji, they were refused. In response, the shrine administrator, {{ill|Kiju Shigekuni|ja|樹下茂国|12=draft}}, along with the Shinto guards and 100 hired farmers, stormed the shrine and destroyed 124 Buddhist statues, artifacts, and scriptures, while confiscating 48 bronze objects, including temple gongs. The seized metal was repurposed for cannon and currency production. This incident set a precedent for Haibutsu Kishaku across Japan. Today, many remaining {{ill|Sannō-sha|ja|山王社|12=draft}} shrines have become {{ill|Hie Shrines|ja|日枝神社|12=draft}} or {{ill|Hiyoshi Shrines|ja|日吉神社|12=draft}}, enshrining {{ill|Ōyamakui-no-Kami|ja|大山咋神|12=draft}} as their principal deity. == Hie Sannō Mandala (日吉山王曼荼羅図) == '''Hie Sannō Mandala''' (日吉山王曼荼羅図) is a mandala depicting the Buddhas and deities enshrined at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|12=draft}}, composed according to the doctrines of {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft}} Buddhism. Based on its format, it can be classified as a {{ill|Miya Mandala|ja|宮曼荼羅|12=draft}}, {{ill|Honjibutsu Mandala|ja|本地仏曼荼羅|12=draft}}, or {{ill|Suijaku Mandala|ja|垂迹神曼荼羅|12=draft}}. === Miya Mandala (宮曼荼羅図) === [[File:Sanno Miya Mandala.jpg|thumb|right|150px|Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala (Muromachi period)
The upper section displays the twenty-one shrine deities, their honjibutsu (Buddhist counterparts), and their {{ill|seed syllables|ja|種子 (密教)|12=draft}}. The lower section provides a bird's-eye view of the twenty-one shrines, depicting two shrines at the summit of {{ill|Mount Hachioji|ja|八王子山|12=draft}} and the shrine complex at the foot of the mountain.]] The Miya Mandala is a depiction of shrine landscapes from an aerial perspective. These mandalas were created so that individuals unable to visit the shrine in person could worship from home as a substitute for pilgrimage. Examples designated as Important Cultural Properties include "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala" (held by the {{ill|Yamato Bunkakan|ja|大和文華館|12=draft}}, Nara City) and "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala" (held by {{ill|Hyakusaiji Temple|ja|百済寺|12=draft}}, Higashiomi City), as well as "Silk-colored Sannō Hōtō Mandala" (held by the [[:en:Ōtani University|Ōtani University Museum]], Kyoto City). === Honjibutsu Mandala (本地仏曼荼羅図) === The Honjibutsu Mandala illustrates the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=draft}} in their original Buddhist forms, depicting them seated as regular Buddhist statues within shrine buildings. === Suijaku Mandala (垂迹神曼荼羅図) === Contrasting with the Honjibutsu Mandala, the Suijaku Mandala depicts the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=draft}} in their Shinto god forms. It portrays a scene where the deities of Mount Hiei gather in a single shrine. Examples designated as Important Cultural Properties include the "Hie Sannō Suijaku Mandala" (held by {{ill|Saikyōji Temple|ja|西教寺|12=draft}}, Otsu City), as well as "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Suijaku Mandala" (held by {{ill|Sannō Lecture|ja|山王講|12=draft}}, Kurano-tsuji Tomokura) and "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Jūzenji Mandala" (held by {{ill|Shinnyo-en|ja|真如苑|12=draft}}, Tokyo). == Honjibutsu of the 21 Sannō Shrines (山王21社の本地仏) == {| class="wikitable" ! !! [[File:Mount Hiei from Kyoto - IMG 5701.JPG|thumb|right|300px|Mount Hiei]] '''Sannō Gongen''' (山王権現) is a {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|12=draft}} deity that emerged from the fusion of {{ill|mountain worship|ja|山岳信仰|12=draft}} on Mount Hiei ({{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|12=draft}}), {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft}} Buddhism. It serves as the {{ill|chinjugami|ja|鎮守神|lt=guardian deity|12=draft}} of the Tendai school. It was also referred to as '''Hiyoshi Gongen''' or '''Hiyoshi Sannō Gongen'''. == History == Sannō Gongen is the {{ill|chinjugami|ja|鎮守神|lt=guardian deity|12=draft}} of {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft}}, formed through the fusion of {{ill|mountain worship|ja|山岳信仰|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft}} on Mount Hiei ({{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|12=draft}}). It is also sometimes used to refer to the {{ill|saijin|ja|祭神|lt=deities enshrined|12=draft}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft}}. Sannō Gongen, as the deity of Mount Hiei, is also affectionately called "Hiyossan" (Hiyoshi-san) and is enshrined in all [[:Category:Hiyoshi Shrines|Hiyoshi Shrines]] nationwide, with {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft}} serving as the head shrine[http://www.rekihaku.otsu.shiga.jp/news/1104.html "Special Exhibition on Kami and Buddhas in Ōmi" (Ōtsu City Museum of History, September 3, 2011)]. The term "Hiyoshi Sannō" refers to the blended faith that regarded Mount Hiei as a "sacred mountain," integrating the belief systems of both {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft}} and {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft}}. At {{ill|Guoqing Temple|ja|国清寺|12=draft}} on {{ill|Mount Tiantai|ja|天台山|12=draft}}, where {{ill|Saichō|ja|最澄|12=draft}} (Dengyō Daishi), the founder of Japanese Tendai Buddhism, studied, the guardian deity was '''Jizhu Sannō Yuanbi Zhenjun''' (地主山王元弼真君), a deified prince of {{ill|King Ling of Zhou|ja|霊王_(周)|12=draft}} in {{ill|Daoism|ja|道教|12=draft}}. When Saichō returned to Japan, he enshrined Sannō Gongen as the guardian deity of {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft}}, modeling it after Guoqing Temple. The peak of {{ill|Otowa Mountain|ja|音羽山_(滋賀県・京都府)|12=draft}}, known as Ushioyama in ancient times, was revered as a sacred place where households offered their first harvest to the gods[http://kanko.city.kyoto.lg.jp/detail.php?InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000415 Kyoto Tourism Navi - Hōgon-ji (Ushio Kannon, Ushioyama)]. This belief laid the foundation for the mountain worship of Hieyama (Mount Hiei). Additionally, the *Kojiki* mentions that [[Oyamakui no Kami]], also called Yamasue-no-Ōnushi-no-Kami, dwells on Mount Hiei (日枝山) and at {{ill|Matsuo Taisha|ja|松尾大社|12=draft}} in {{ill|Kadono District|ja|葛野郡|12=draft}}. Furthermore, from {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社|12=draft}}, where {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主|12=draft}} is worshipped as the *nigi-mitama* (gentle spirit) of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴神|12=draft}}, the deity was enshrined at Mount Hiei. This process established {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft}}, making it one of the most syncretic shrines, serving as the guardian deity of the Tendai school and its temple complex[https://www.asahi.com/travel/hikyou/TKY201103270195.html "Japan's Tradition: Shinbutsu-shūgō (2)" - Asahi Shimbun Digital, March 28, 2011]. {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft}} maintained a close relationship, with Enryaku-ji holding authority over appointments within Hiyoshi Taisha from the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}} onward. As Tendai Buddhism spread throughout Japan, temples dedicated to Sannō Gongen, called '''Sannō-sha''', were established across the country. In addition to Sannō Gongen, Tendai Buddhism also enshrined {{ill|Hachioji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen|12=draft}} at Mount Hiei and assigned {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹|12=draft}} Buddhas to the 21 Sannō Shrines. At the beginning of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, the shrine priests of Hiyoshi Taisha attempted to abolish Shinbutsu-shūgō and establish a form of exclusive Shinto known as {{ill|Yoshida Shintō|ja|唯一神道|Yoshida Shintō=Yoshida Shintō|lt=Ikkō Shintō|12=draft}}. This led them to burn Buddhist images and engage in an early attempt at {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|12=draft}}, the movement to eliminate Buddhism. However, Enryaku-ji appealed to the shogunate, resulting in a court ruling against Hiyoshi Taisha. The shrine priests were exiled, and the shrine was placed under complete control of Enryaku-ji. During this period, Enryaku-ji established a rule that "any actions beyond festivals and cleaning must receive permission from Enryaku-ji". From that point on, Hiyoshi Taisha was also financially dependent on Enryaku-ji. == Separation of Kami and Buddhas / Haibutsu Kishaku == During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, the government implemented {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}}, leading to the abolition of Sannō Gongen, the guardian deity of Tendai Buddhism. Hiyoshi Taisha was one of the first sites to undergo the {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離令|12=draft}} in {{ill|Keiō|ja|慶応|12=draft}} 4 (1868). Hiyoshi Sannō Shrine was forcibly separated from Enryaku-jiBecause the Shinto faction engaged in aggressive destruction of Buddhist-related objects, the new government issued a directive on April 10, Keiō 4, to implement Shinbutsu Bunri more peacefully. and reorganized into {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft}}. When the shrine attempted to take custody of its buildings' keys from Enryaku-ji, they were refused. In response, the shrine administrator, {{ill|Kiju Shigekuni|ja|樹下茂国|12=draft}}, along with the Shinto guards and 100 hired farmers, stormed the shrine and destroyed 124 Buddhist statues, artifacts, and scriptures, while confiscating 48 bronze objects, including temple gongs. The seized metal was repurposed for cannon and currency production. This incident set a precedent for Haibutsu Kishaku across Japan. Today, many remaining {{ill|Sannō-sha|ja|山王社|12=draft}} shrines have become {{ill|Hie Shrines|ja|日枝神社|12=draft}} or {{ill|Hiyoshi Shrines|ja|日吉神社|12=draft}}, enshrining {{ill|Ōyamakui-no-Kami|ja|大山咋神|12=draft}} as their principal deity. == Hie Sannō Mandala (日吉山王曼荼羅図) == '''Hie Sannō Mandala''' (日吉山王曼荼羅図) is a mandala depicting the Buddhas and deities enshrined at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|12=draft}}, composed according to the doctrines of {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft}} Buddhism. Based on its format, it can be classified as a {{ill|Miya Mandala|ja|宮曼荼羅|12=draft}}, {{ill|Honjibutsu Mandala|ja|本地仏曼荼羅|12=draft}}, or {{ill|Suijaku Mandala|ja|垂迹神曼荼羅|12=draft}}. === Miya Mandala (宮曼荼羅図) === [[File:Sanno Miya Mandala.jpg|thumb|right|150px|Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala (Muromachi period)
The upper section displays the twenty-one shrine deities, their honjibutsu (Buddhist counterparts), and their {{ill|seed syllables|ja|種子 (密教)|12=draft}}. The lower section provides a bird's-eye view of the twenty-one shrines, depicting two shrines at the summit of {{ill|Mount Hachioji|ja|八王子山|12=draft}} and the shrine complex at the foot of the mountain.]] The Miya Mandala is a depiction of shrine landscapes from an aerial perspective. These mandalas were created so that individuals unable to visit the shrine in person could worship from home as a substitute for pilgrimage. Examples designated as Important Cultural Properties include "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala" (held by the {{ill|Yamato Bunkakan|ja|大和文華館|12=draft}}, Nara City) and "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala" (held by {{ill|Hyakusaiji Temple|ja|百済寺|12=draft}}, Higashiomi City), as well as "Silk-colored Sannō Hōtō Mandala" (held by the [[:en:Ōtani University|Ōtani University Museum]], Kyoto City). === Honjibutsu Mandala (本地仏曼荼羅図) === The Honjibutsu Mandala illustrates the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=draft}} in their original Buddhist forms, depicting them seated as regular Buddhist statues within shrine buildings. === Suijaku Mandala (垂迹神曼荼羅図) === Contrasting with the Honjibutsu Mandala, the Suijaku Mandala depicts the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=draft}} in their Shinto god forms. It portrays a scene where the deities of Mount Hiei gather in a single shrine. Examples designated as Important Cultural Properties include the "Hie Sannō Suijaku Mandala" (held by {{ill|Saikyōji Temple|ja|西教寺|12=draft}}, Otsu City), as well as "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Suijaku Mandala" (held by {{ill|Sannō Lecture|ja|山王講|12=draft}}, Kurano-tsuji Tomokura) and "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Jūzenji Mandala" (held by {{ill|Shinnyo-en|ja|真如苑|12=draft}}, Tokyo). == Honjibutsu of the 21 Sannō Shrines (山王21社の本地仏) == {| class="wikitable" ! !! It was also referred to as '''Hiyoshi Gongen''' or '''Hiyoshi Sannō Gongen'''. == History == Sannō Gongen is the {{ill|chinjugami|ja|鎮守神|lt=guardian deity|12=draft}} of {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft}}, formed through the fusion of {{ill|mountain worship|ja|山岳信仰|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft}} on Mount Hiei ({{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|12=draft}}). It is also sometimes used to refer to the {{ill|saijin|ja|祭神|lt=deities enshrined|12=draft}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft}}. Sannō Gongen, as the deity of Mount Hiei, is also affectionately called "Hiyossan" (Hiyoshi-san) and is enshrined in all [[:Category:Hiyoshi Shrines|Hiyoshi Shrines]] nationwide, with {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft}} serving as the head shrine[http://www.rekihaku.otsu.shiga.jp/news/1104.html "Special Exhibition on Kami and Buddhas in Ōmi" (Ōtsu City Museum of History, September 3, 2011)]. The term "Hiyoshi Sannō" refers to the blended faith that regarded Mount Hiei as a "sacred mountain," integrating the belief systems of both {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft}} and {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft}}. At {{ill|Guoqing Temple|ja|国清寺|12=draft}} on {{ill|Mount Tiantai|ja|天台山|12=draft}}, where {{ill|Saichō|ja|最澄|12=draft}} (Dengyō Daishi), the founder of Japanese Tendai Buddhism, studied, the guardian deity was '''Jizhu Sannō Yuanbi Zhenjun''' (地主山王元弼真君), a deified prince of {{ill|King Ling of Zhou|ja|霊王_(周)|12=draft}} in {{ill|Daoism|ja|道教|12=draft}}. When Saichō returned to Japan, he enshrined Sannō Gongen as the guardian deity of {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft}}, modeling it after Guoqing Temple. The peak of {{ill|Otowa Mountain|ja|音羽山_(滋賀県・京都府)|12=draft}}, known as Ushioyama in ancient times, was revered as a sacred place where households offered their first harvest to the gods[http://kanko.city.kyoto.lg.jp/detail.php?InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000415 Kyoto Tourism Navi - Hōgon-ji (Ushio Kannon, Ushioyama)]. This belief laid the foundation for the mountain worship of Hieyama (Mount Hiei). Additionally, the *Kojiki* mentions that [[Oyamakui no Kami]], also called Yamasue-no-Ōnushi-no-Kami, dwells on Mount Hiei (日枝山) and at {{ill|Matsuo Taisha|ja|松尾大社|12=draft}} in {{ill|Kadono District|ja|葛野郡|12=draft}}. Furthermore, from {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社|12=draft}}, where {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主|12=draft}} is worshipped as the *nigi-mitama* (gentle spirit) of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴神|12=draft}}, the deity was enshrined at Mount Hiei. This process established {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft}}, making it one of the most syncretic shrines, serving as the guardian deity of the Tendai school and its temple complex[https://www.asahi.com/travel/hikyou/TKY201103270195.html "Japan's Tradition: Shinbutsu-shūgō (2)" - Asahi Shimbun Digital, March 28, 2011]. {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft}} maintained a close relationship, with Enryaku-ji holding authority over appointments within Hiyoshi Taisha from the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}} onward. As Tendai Buddhism spread throughout Japan, temples dedicated to Sannō Gongen, called '''Sannō-sha''', were established across the country. In addition to Sannō Gongen, Tendai Buddhism also enshrined {{ill|Hachioji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen|12=draft}} at Mount Hiei and assigned {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹|12=draft}} Buddhas to the 21 Sannō Shrines. At the beginning of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, the shrine priests of Hiyoshi Taisha attempted to abolish Shinbutsu-shūgō and establish a form of exclusive Shinto known as {{ill|Yoshida Shintō|ja|唯一神道|Yoshida Shintō=Yoshida Shintō|lt=Ikkō Shintō|12=draft}}. This led them to burn Buddhist images and engage in an early attempt at {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|12=draft}}, the movement to eliminate Buddhism. However, Enryaku-ji appealed to the shogunate, resulting in a court ruling against Hiyoshi Taisha. The shrine priests were exiled, and the shrine was placed under complete control of Enryaku-ji. During this period, Enryaku-ji established a rule that "any actions beyond festivals and cleaning must receive permission from Enryaku-ji". From that point on, Hiyoshi Taisha was also financially dependent on Enryaku-ji. == Separation of Kami and Buddhas / Haibutsu Kishaku == During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, the government implemented {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}}, leading to the abolition of Sannō Gongen, the guardian deity of Tendai Buddhism. Hiyoshi Taisha was one of the first sites to undergo the {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離令|12=draft}} in {{ill|Keiō|ja|慶応|12=draft}} 4 (1868). Hiyoshi Sannō Shrine was forcibly separated from Enryaku-jiBecause the Shinto faction engaged in aggressive destruction of Buddhist-related objects, the new government issued a directive on April 10, Keiō 4, to implement Shinbutsu Bunri more peacefully. and reorganized into {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft}}. When the shrine attempted to take custody of its buildings' keys from Enryaku-ji, they were refused. In response, the shrine administrator, {{ill|Kiju Shigekuni|ja|樹下茂国|12=draft}}, along with the Shinto guards and 100 hired farmers, stormed the shrine and destroyed 124 Buddhist statues, artifacts, and scriptures, while confiscating 48 bronze objects, including temple gongs. The seized metal was repurposed for cannon and currency production. This incident set a precedent for Haibutsu Kishaku across Japan. Today, many remaining {{ill|Sannō-sha|ja|山王社|12=draft}} shrines have become {{ill|Hie Shrines|ja|日枝神社|12=draft}} or {{ill|Hiyoshi Shrines|ja|日吉神社|12=draft}}, enshrining {{ill|Ōyamakui-no-Kami|ja|大山咋神|12=draft}} as their principal deity. == Hie Sannō Mandala (日吉山王曼荼羅図) == '''Hie Sannō Mandala''' (日吉山王曼荼羅図) is a mandala depicting the Buddhas and deities enshrined at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|12=draft}}, composed according to the doctrines of {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft}} Buddhism. Based on its format, it can be classified as a {{ill|Miya Mandala|ja|宮曼荼羅|12=draft}}, {{ill|Honjibutsu Mandala|ja|本地仏曼荼羅|12=draft}}, or {{ill|Suijaku Mandala|ja|垂迹神曼荼羅|12=draft}}. === Miya Mandala (宮曼荼羅図) === [[File:Sanno Miya Mandala.jpg|thumb|right|150px|Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala (Muromachi period)
The upper section displays the twenty-one shrine deities, their honjibutsu (Buddhist counterparts), and their {{ill|seed syllables|ja|種子 (密教)|12=draft}}. The lower section provides a bird's-eye view of the twenty-one shrines, depicting two shrines at the summit of {{ill|Mount Hachioji|ja|八王子山|12=draft}} and the shrine complex at the foot of the mountain.]] The Miya Mandala is a depiction of shrine landscapes from an aerial perspective. These mandalas were created so that individuals unable to visit the shrine in person could worship from home as a substitute for pilgrimage. Examples designated as Important Cultural Properties include "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala" (held by the {{ill|Yamato Bunkakan|ja|大和文華館|12=draft}}, Nara City) and "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala" (held by {{ill|Hyakusaiji Temple|ja|百済寺|12=draft}}, Higashiomi City), as well as "Silk-colored Sannō Hōtō Mandala" (held by the [[:en:Ōtani University|Ōtani University Museum]], Kyoto City). === Honjibutsu Mandala (本地仏曼荼羅図) === The Honjibutsu Mandala illustrates the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=draft}} in their original Buddhist forms, depicting them seated as regular Buddhist statues within shrine buildings. === Suijaku Mandala (垂迹神曼荼羅図) === Contrasting with the Honjibutsu Mandala, the Suijaku Mandala depicts the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=draft}} in their Shinto god forms. It portrays a scene where the deities of Mount Hiei gather in a single shrine. Examples designated as Important Cultural Properties include the "Hie Sannō Suijaku Mandala" (held by {{ill|Saikyōji Temple|ja|西教寺|12=draft}}, Otsu City), as well as "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Suijaku Mandala" (held by {{ill|Sannō Lecture|ja|山王講|12=draft}}, Kurano-tsuji Tomokura) and "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Jūzenji Mandala" (held by {{ill|Shinnyo-en|ja|真如苑|12=draft}}, Tokyo). == Honjibutsu of the 21 Sannō Shrines (山王21社の本地仏) == {| class="wikitable" ! !! revision.text:
[[File:Mount Hiei from Kyoto - IMG 5701.JPG|thumb|right|300px|Mount Hiei]] '''Sannō Gongen''' (山王権現) is a {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|12=draft}} deity that emerged from the fusion of {{ill|mountain worship|ja|山岳信仰|12=draft}} on Mount Hiei ({{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|12=draft}}), {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft}} Buddhism. It serves as the {{ill|chinjugami|ja|鎮守神|lt=guardian deity|12=draft}} of the Tendai school. It was also referred to as '''Hiyoshi Gongen''' or '''Hiyoshi Sannō Gongen'''. == History == Sannō Gongen is the {{ill|chinjugami|ja|鎮守神|lt=guardian deity|12=draft}} of {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft}}, formed through the fusion of {{ill|mountain worship|ja|山岳信仰|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft}} on Mount Hiei ({{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|12=draft}}). It is also sometimes used to refer to the {{ill|saijin|ja|祭神|lt=deities enshrined|12=draft}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft}}. Sannō Gongen, as the deity of Mount Hiei, is also affectionately called "Hiyossan" (Hiyoshi-san) and is enshrined in all [[:Category:Hiyoshi Shrines|Hiyoshi Shrines]] nationwide, with {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft}} serving as the head shrine[http://www.rekihaku.otsu.shiga.jp/news/1104.html "Special Exhibition on Kami and Buddhas in Ōmi" (Ōtsu City Museum of History, September 3, 2011)]. The term "Hiyoshi Sannō" refers to the blended faith that regarded Mount Hiei as a "sacred mountain," integrating the belief systems of both {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft}} and {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft}}. At {{ill|Guoqing Temple|ja|国清寺|12=draft}} on {{ill|Mount Tiantai|ja|天台山|12=draft}}, where {{ill|Saichō|ja|最澄|12=draft}} (Dengyō Daishi), the founder of Japanese Tendai Buddhism, studied, the guardian deity was '''Jizhu Sannō Yuanbi Zhenjun''' (地主山王元弼真君), a deified prince of {{ill|King Ling of Zhou|ja|霊王_(周)|12=draft}} in {{ill|Daoism|ja|道教|12=draft}}. When Saichō returned to Japan, he enshrined Sannō Gongen as the guardian deity of {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft}}, modeling it after Guoqing Temple. The peak of {{ill|Otowa Mountain|ja|音羽山_(滋賀県・京都府)|12=draft}}, known as Ushioyama in ancient times, was revered as a sacred place where households offered their first harvest to the gods[http://kanko.city.kyoto.lg.jp/detail.php?InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000415 Kyoto Tourism Navi - Hōgon-ji (Ushio Kannon, Ushioyama)]. This belief laid the foundation for the mountain worship of Hieyama (Mount Hiei). Additionally, the *Kojiki* mentions that [[Oyamakui no Kami]], also called Yamasue-no-Ōnushi-no-Kami, dwells on Mount Hiei (日枝山) and at {{ill|Matsuo Taisha|ja|松尾大社|12=draft}} in {{ill|Kadono District|ja|葛野郡|12=draft}}. Furthermore, from {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社|12=draft}}, where {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主|12=draft}} is worshipped as the *nigi-mitama* (gentle spirit) of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴神|12=draft}}, the deity was enshrined at Mount Hiei. This process established {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft}}, making it one of the most syncretic shrines, serving as the guardian deity of the Tendai school and its temple complex[https://www.asahi.com/travel/hikyou/TKY201103270195.html "Japan's Tradition: Shinbutsu-shūgō (2)" - Asahi Shimbun Digital, March 28, 2011]. {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft}} maintained a close relationship, with Enryaku-ji holding authority over appointments within Hiyoshi Taisha from the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}} onward. As Tendai Buddhism spread throughout Japan, temples dedicated to Sannō Gongen, called '''Sannō-sha''', were established across the country. In addition to Sannō Gongen, Tendai Buddhism also enshrined {{ill|Hachioji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen|12=draft}} at Mount Hiei and assigned {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹|12=draft}} Buddhas to the 21 Sannō Shrines. At the beginning of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, the shrine priests of Hiyoshi Taisha attempted to abolish Shinbutsu-shūgō and establish a form of exclusive Shinto known as {{ill|Yoshida Shintō|ja|唯一神道|Yoshida Shintō=Yoshida Shintō|lt=Ikkō Shintō|12=draft}}. This led them to burn Buddhist images and engage in an early attempt at {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|12=draft}}, the movement to eliminate Buddhism. However, Enryaku-ji appealed to the shogunate, resulting in a court ruling against Hiyoshi Taisha. The shrine priests were exiled, and the shrine was placed under complete control of Enryaku-ji. During this period, Enryaku-ji established a rule that "any actions beyond festivals and cleaning must receive permission from Enryaku-ji". From that point on, Hiyoshi Taisha was also financially dependent on Enryaku-ji. == Separation of Kami and Buddhas / Haibutsu Kishaku == During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, the government implemented {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}}, leading to the abolition of Sannō Gongen, the guardian deity of Tendai Buddhism. Hiyoshi Taisha was one of the first sites to undergo the {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離令|12=draft}} in {{ill|Keiō|ja|慶応|12=draft}} 4 (1868). Hiyoshi Sannō Shrine was forcibly separated from Enryaku-jiBecause the Shinto faction engaged in aggressive destruction of Buddhist-related objects, the new government issued a directive on April 10, Keiō 4, to implement Shinbutsu Bunri more peacefully. and reorganized into {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft}}. When the shrine attempted to take custody of its buildings' keys from Enryaku-ji, they were refused. In response, the shrine administrator, {{ill|Kiju Shigekuni|ja|樹下茂国|12=draft}}, along with the Shinto guards and 100 hired farmers, stormed the shrine and destroyed 124 Buddhist statues, artifacts, and scriptures, while confiscating 48 bronze objects, including temple gongs. The seized metal was repurposed for cannon and currency production. This incident set a precedent for Haibutsu Kishaku across Japan. Today, many remaining {{ill|Sannō-sha|ja|山王社|12=draft}} shrines have become {{ill|Hie Shrines|ja|日枝神社|12=draft}} or {{ill|Hiyoshi Shrines|ja|日吉神社|12=draft}}, enshrining {{ill|Ōyamakui-no-Kami|ja|大山咋神|12=draft}} as their principal deity. == Hie Sannō Mandala (日吉山王曼荼羅図) == '''Hie Sannō Mandala''' (日吉山王曼荼羅図) is a mandala depicting the Buddhas and deities enshrined at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|12=draft}}, composed according to the doctrines of {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft}} Buddhism. Based on its format, it can be classified as a {{ill|Miya Mandala|ja|宮曼荼羅|12=draft}}, {{ill|Honjibutsu Mandala|ja|本地仏曼荼羅|12=draft}}, or {{ill|Suijaku Mandala|ja|垂迹神曼荼羅|12=draft}}. === Miya Mandala (宮曼荼羅図) === [[File:Sanno Miya Mandala.jpg|thumb|right|150px|Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala (Muromachi period)
The upper section displays the twenty-one shrine deities, their honjibutsu (Buddhist counterparts), and their {{ill|seed syllables|ja|種子 (密教)|12=draft}}. The lower section provides a bird's-eye view of the twenty-one shrines, depicting two shrines at the summit of {{ill|Mount Hachioji|ja|八王子山|12=draft}} and the shrine complex at the foot of the mountain.]] The Miya Mandala is a depiction of shrine landscapes from an aerial perspective. These mandalas were created so that individuals unable to visit the shrine in person could worship from home as a substitute for pilgrimage. Examples designated as Important Cultural Properties include "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala" (held by the {{ill|Yamato Bunkakan|ja|大和文華館|12=draft}}, Nara City) and "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala" (held by {{ill|Hyakusaiji Temple|ja|百済寺|12=draft}}, Higashiomi City), as well as "Silk-colored Sannō Hōtō Mandala" (held by the [[:en:Ōtani University|Ōtani University Museum]], Kyoto City). === Honjibutsu Mandala (本地仏曼荼羅図) === The Honjibutsu Mandala illustrates the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=draft}} in their original Buddhist forms, depicting them seated as regular Buddhist statues within shrine buildings. === Suijaku Mandala (垂迹神曼荼羅図) === Contrasting with the Honjibutsu Mandala, the Suijaku Mandala depicts the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=draft}} in their Shinto god forms. It portrays a scene where the deities of Mount Hiei gather in a single shrine. Examples designated as Important Cultural Properties include the "Hie Sannō Suijaku Mandala" (held by {{ill|Saikyōji Temple|ja|西教寺|12=draft}}, Otsu City), as well as "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Suijaku Mandala" (held by {{ill|Sannō Lecture|ja|山王講|12=draft}}, Kurano-tsuji Tomokura) and "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Jūzenji Mandala" (held by {{ill|Shinnyo-en|ja|真如苑|12=draft}}, Tokyo). == Honjibutsu of the 21 Sannō Shrines (山王21社の本地仏) == {| class="wikitable" ! !! Former Name !! Honjibutsu !! Location |- |rowspan=7|Upper Seven Shrines
({{ill|Sannō Seven Shrines|ja|山王七社|12=draft}}) |Dai-gu (Great Hiei)||{{ill|Shakyamuni Buddha|ja|釈迦如来|12=draft}}|| |- |Ninomiya (Small Hiei)||{{ill|Medicine Buddha|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi The seized metal was repurposed for cannon and currency production. This incident set a precedent for Haibutsu Kishaku across Japan. Today, many remaining {{ill|Sannō-sha|ja|山王社|12=draft}} shrines have become {{ill|Hie Shrines|ja|日枝神社|12=draft}} or {{ill|Hiyoshi Shrines|ja|日吉神社|12=draft}}, enshrining {{ill|Ōyamakui-no-Kami|ja|大山咋神|12=draft}} as their principal deity. == Hie Sannō Mandala (日吉山王曼荼羅図) == '''Hie Sannō Mandala''' (日吉山王曼荼羅図) is a mandala depicting the Buddhas and deities enshrined at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|12=draft}}, composed according to the doctrines of {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft}} Buddhism. Based on its format, it can be classified as a {{ill|Miya Mandala|ja|宮曼荼羅|12=draft}}, {{ill|Honjibutsu Mandala|ja|本地仏曼荼羅|12=draft}}, or {{ill|Suijaku Mandala|ja|垂迹神曼荼羅|12=draft}}. === Miya Mandala (宮曼荼羅図) === [[File:Sanno Miya Mandala.jpg|thumb|right|150px|Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala (Muromachi period)
The upper section displays the twenty-one shrine deities, their honjibutsu (Buddhist counterparts), and their {{ill|seed syllables|ja|種子 (密教)|12=draft}}. The lower section provides a bird's-eye view of the twenty-one shrines, depicting two shrines at the summit of {{ill|Mount Hachioji|ja|八王子山|12=draft}} and the shrine complex at the foot of the mountain.]] The Miya Mandala is a depiction of shrine landscapes from an aerial perspective. These mandalas were created so that individuals unable to visit the shrine in person could worship from home as a substitute for pilgrimage. Examples designated as Important Cultural Properties include "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala" (held by the {{ill|Yamato Bunkakan|ja|大和文華館|12=draft}}, Nara City) and "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala" (held by {{ill|Hyakusaiji Temple|ja|百済寺|12=draft}}, Higashiomi City), as well as "Silk-colored Sannō Hōtō Mandala" (held by the [[:en:Ōtani University|Ōtani University Museum]], Kyoto City). === Honjibutsu Mandala (本地仏曼荼羅図) === The Honjibutsu Mandala illustrates the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=draft}} in their original Buddhist forms, depicting them seated as regular Buddhist statues within shrine buildings. === Suijaku Mandala (垂迹神曼荼羅図) === Contrasting with the Honjibutsu Mandala, the Suijaku Mandala depicts the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=draft}} in their Shinto god forms. It portrays a scene where the deities of Mount Hiei gather in a single shrine. Examples designated as Important Cultural Properties include the "Hie Sannō Suijaku Mandala" (held by {{ill|Saikyōji Temple|ja|西教寺|12=draft}}, Otsu City), as well as "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Suijaku Mandala" (held by {{ill|Sannō Lecture|ja|山王講|12=draft}}, Kurano-tsuji Tomokura) and "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Jūzenji Mandala" (held by {{ill|Shinnyo-en|ja|真如苑|12=draft}}, Tokyo). == Honjibutsu of the 21 Sannō Shrines (山王21社の本地仏) == {| class="wikitable" ! !! Former Name !! Honjibutsu !! Location |- |rowspan=7|Upper Seven Shrines

revision.text:
[[File:Mount Hiei from Kyoto - IMG 5701.JPG|thumb|right|300px|Mount Hiei]] '''Sannō Gongen''' (山王権現) is a {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|12=draft|12=draft}} deity that emerged from the fusion of {{ill|mountain worship|ja|山岳信仰|12=draft|12=draft}} on Mount Hiei ({{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|12=draft|12=draft}}), {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft|12=draft}} Buddhism. It serves as the {{ill|chinjugami|ja|鎮守神|lt=guardian deity|12=draft|12=draft}} of the Tendai school. It was also referred to as '''Hiyoshi Gongen''' or '''Hiyoshi Sannō Gongen'''. == History == Sannō Gongen is the {{ill|chinjugami|ja|鎮守神|lt=guardian deity|12=draft|12=draft}} of {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft|12=draft}}, formed through the fusion of {{ill|mountain worship|ja|山岳信仰|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft|12=draft}} on Mount Hiei ({{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|12=draft|12=draft}}). It is also sometimes used to refer to the {{ill|saijin|ja|祭神|lt=deities enshrined|12=draft|12=draft}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft|12=draft}}. Sannō Gongen, as the deity of Mount Hiei, is also affectionately called "Hiyossan" (Hiyoshi-san) and is enshrined in all [[:Category:Hiyoshi Shrines|Hiyoshi Shrines]] nationwide, with {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft|12=draft}} serving as the head shrine[http://www.rekihaku.otsu.shiga.jp/news/1104.html "Special Exhibition on Kami and Buddhas in Ōmi" (Ōtsu City Museum of History, September 3, 2011)]. The term "Hiyoshi Sannō" refers to the blended faith that regarded Mount Hiei as a "sacred mountain," integrating the belief systems of both {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft|12=draft}}. At {{ill|Guoqing Temple|ja|国清寺|12=draft|12=draft}} on {{ill|Mount Tiantai|ja|天台山|12=draft|12=draft}}, where {{ill|Saichō|ja|最澄|12=draft|12=draft}} (Dengyō Daishi), the founder of Japanese Tendai Buddhism, studied, the guardian deity was '''Jizhu Sannō Yuanbi Zhenjun''' (地主山王元弼真君), a deified prince of {{ill|King Ling of Zhou|ja|霊王_(周)|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Daoism|ja|道教|12=draft|12=draft}}. When Saichō returned to Japan, he enshrined Sannō Gongen as the guardian deity of {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft|12=draft}}, modeling it after Guoqing Temple. The peak of {{ill|Otowa Mountain|ja|音羽山_(滋賀県・京都府)|12=draft|12=draft}}, known as Ushioyama in ancient times, was revered as a sacred place where households offered their first harvest to the gods[http://kanko.city.kyoto.lg.jp/detail.php?InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000415 Kyoto Tourism Navi - Hōgon-ji (Ushio Kannon, Ushioyama)]. This belief laid the foundation for the mountain worship of Hieyama (Mount Hiei). Additionally, the *Kojiki* mentions that [[Oyamakui no Kami]], also called Yamasue-no-Ōnushi-no-Kami, dwells on Mount Hiei (日枝山) and at {{ill|Matsuo Taisha|ja|松尾大社|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Kadono District|ja|葛野郡|12=draft|12=draft}}. Furthermore, from {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, where {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主|12=draft|12=draft}} is worshipped as the *nigi-mitama* (gentle spirit) of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴神|12=draft|12=draft}}, the deity was enshrined at Mount Hiei. This process established {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft|12=draft}}, making it one of the most syncretic shrines, serving as the guardian deity of the Tendai school and its temple complex[https://www.asahi.com/travel/hikyou/TKY201103270195.html "Japan's Tradition: Shinbutsu-shūgō (2)" - Asahi Shimbun Digital, March 28, 2011]. {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft|12=draft}} maintained a close relationship, with Enryaku-ji holding authority over appointments within Hiyoshi Taisha from the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft|12=draft}} onward. As Tendai Buddhism spread throughout Japan, temples dedicated to Sannō Gongen, called '''Sannō-sha''', were established across the country. In addition to Sannō Gongen, Tendai Buddhism also enshrined {{ill|Hachioji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen|12=draft|12=draft}} at Mount Hiei and assigned {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹|12=draft|12=draft}} Buddhas to the 21 Sannō Shrines. At the beginning of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the shrine priests of Hiyoshi Taisha attempted to abolish Shinbutsu-shūgō and establish a form of exclusive Shinto known as {{ill|Yoshida Shintō|ja|唯一神道|Yoshida Shintō=Yoshida Shintō|lt=Ikkō Shintō|12=draft|12=draft}}. This led them to burn Buddhist images and engage in an early attempt at {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|12=draft|12=draft}}, the movement to eliminate Buddhism. However, Enryaku-ji appealed to the shogunate, resulting in a court ruling against Hiyoshi Taisha. The shrine priests were exiled, and the shrine was placed under complete control of Enryaku-ji. During this period, Enryaku-ji established a rule that "any actions beyond festivals and cleaning must receive permission from Enryaku-ji". From that point on, Hiyoshi Taisha was also financially dependent on Enryaku-ji. == Separation of Kami and Buddhas / Haibutsu Kishaku == During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft|12=draft}}, the government implemented {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft|12=draft}}, leading to the abolition of Sannō Gongen, the guardian deity of Tendai Buddhism. Hiyoshi Taisha was one of the first sites to undergo the {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離令|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Keiō|ja|慶応|12=draft|12=draft}} 4 (1868). Hiyoshi Sannō Shrine was forcibly separated from Enryaku-jiBecause the Shinto faction engaged in aggressive destruction of Buddhist-related objects, the new government issued a directive on April 10, Keiō 4, to implement Shinbutsu Bunri more peacefully. and reorganized into {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft|12=draft}}. When the shrine attempted to take custody of its buildings' keys from Enryaku-ji, they were refused. In response, the shrine administrator, {{ill|Kiju Shigekuni|ja|樹下茂国|12=draft|12=draft}}, along with the Shinto guards and 100 hired farmers, stormed the shrine and destroyed 124 Buddhist statues, artifacts, and scriptures, while confiscating 48 bronze objects, including temple gongs. The seized metal was repurposed for cannon and currency production. This incident set a precedent for Haibutsu Kishaku across Japan. Today, many remaining {{ill|Sannō-sha|ja|山王社|12=draft|12=draft}} shrines have become {{ill|Hie Shrines|ja|日枝神社|12=draft|12=draft}} or {{ill|Hiyoshi Shrines|ja|日吉神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, enshrining {{ill|Ōyamakui-no-Kami|ja|大山咋神|12=draft|12=draft}} as their principal deity. == Hie Sannō Mandala (日吉山王曼荼羅図) == '''Hie Sannō Mandala''' (日吉山王曼荼羅図) is a mandala depicting the Buddhas and deities enshrined at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|12=draft|12=draft}}, composed according to the doctrines of {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft|12=draft}} Buddhism. Based on its format, it can be classified as a {{ill|Miya Mandala|ja|宮曼荼羅|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Honjibutsu Mandala|ja|本地仏曼荼羅|12=draft|12=draft}}, or {{ill|Suijaku Mandala|ja|垂迹神曼荼羅|12=draft|12=draft}}. === Miya Mandala (宮曼荼羅図) === [[File:Sanno Miya Mandala.jpg|thumb|right|150px|Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala (Muromachi period)
The upper section displays the twenty-one shrine deities, their honjibutsu (Buddhist counterparts), and their {{ill|seed syllables|ja|種子 (密教)|12=draft|12=draft}}. The lower section provides a bird's-eye view of the twenty-one shrines, depicting two shrines at the summit of {{ill|Mount Hachioji|ja|八王子山|12=draft|12=draft}} and the shrine complex at the foot of the mountain.]] The Miya Mandala is a depiction of shrine landscapes from an aerial perspective. These mandalas were created so that individuals unable to visit the shrine in person could worship from home as a substitute for pilgrimage. Examples designated as Important Cultural Properties include "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala" (held by the {{ill|Yamato Bunkakan|ja|大和文華館|12=draft|12=draft}}, Nara City) and "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala" (held by {{ill|Hyakusaiji Temple|ja|百済寺|12=draft|12=draft}}, Higashiomi City), as well as "Silk-colored Sannō Hōtō Mandala" (held by the [[:en:Ōtani University|Ōtani University Museum]], Kyoto City). === Honjibutsu Mandala (本地仏曼荼羅図) === The Honjibutsu Mandala illustrates the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=draft|12=draft}} in their original Buddhist forms, depicting them seated as regular Buddhist statues within shrine buildings. === Suijaku Mandala (垂迹神曼荼羅図) === Contrasting with the Honjibutsu Mandala, the Suijaku Mandala depicts the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=draft|12=draft}} in their Shinto god forms. It portrays a scene where the deities of Mount Hiei gather in a single shrine. Examples designated as Important Cultural Properties include the "Hie Sannō Suijaku Mandala" (held by {{ill|Saikyōji Temple|ja|西教寺|12=draft|12=draft}}, Otsu City), as well as "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Suijaku Mandala" (held by {{ill|Sannō Lecture|ja|山王講|12=draft|12=draft}}, Kurano-tsuji Tomokura) and "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Jūzenji Mandala" (held by {{ill|Shinnyo-en|ja|真如苑|12=draft|12=draft}}, Tokyo). == Honjibutsu of the 21 Sannō Shrines (山王21社の本地仏) == {| class="wikitable" ! !! revision.text:
[[File:Mount Hiei from Kyoto - IMG 5701.JPG|thumb|right|300px|Mount Hiei]] '''Sannō Gongen''' (山王権現) is a {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|12=draft|12=draft}} deity that emerged from the fusion of {{ill|mountain worship|ja|山岳信仰|12=draft|12=draft}} on Mount Hiei ({{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|12=draft|12=draft}}), {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft|12=draft}} Buddhism. It serves as the {{ill|chinjugami|ja|鎮守神|lt=guardian deity|12=draft|12=draft}} of the Tendai school. It was also referred to as '''Hiyoshi Gongen''' or '''Hiyoshi Sannō Gongen'''. == History == Sannō Gongen is the {{ill|chinjugami|ja|鎮守神|lt=guardian deity|12=draft|12=draft}} of {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft|12=draft}}, formed through the fusion of {{ill|mountain worship|ja|山岳信仰|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft|12=draft}} on Mount Hiei ({{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|12=draft|12=draft}}). It is also sometimes used to refer to the {{ill|saijin|ja|祭神|lt=deities enshrined|12=draft|12=draft}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft|12=draft}}. Sannō Gongen, as the deity of Mount Hiei, is also affectionately called "Hiyossan" (Hiyoshi-san) and is enshrined in all [[:Category:Hiyoshi Shrines|Hiyoshi Shrines]] nationwide, with {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft|12=draft}} serving as the head shrine[http://www.rekihaku.otsu.shiga.jp/news/1104.html "Special Exhibition on Kami and Buddhas in Ōmi" (Ōtsu City Museum of History, September 3, 2011)]. The term "Hiyoshi Sannō" refers to the blended faith that regarded Mount Hiei as a "sacred mountain," integrating the belief systems of both {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft|12=draft}}. At {{ill|Guoqing Temple|ja|国清寺|12=draft|12=draft}} on {{ill|Mount Tiantai|ja|天台山|12=draft|12=draft}}, where {{ill|Saichō|ja|最澄|12=draft|12=draft}} (Dengyō Daishi), the founder of Japanese Tendai Buddhism, studied, the guardian deity was '''Jizhu Sannō Yuanbi Zhenjun''' (地主山王元弼真君), a deified prince of {{ill|King Ling of Zhou|ja|霊王_(周)|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Daoism|ja|道教|12=draft|12=draft}}. When Saichō returned to Japan, he enshrined Sannō Gongen as the guardian deity of {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft|12=draft}}, modeling it after Guoqing Temple. The peak of {{ill|Otowa Mountain|ja|音羽山_(滋賀県・京都府)|12=draft|12=draft}}, known as Ushioyama in ancient times, was revered as a sacred place where households offered their first harvest to the gods[http://kanko.city.kyoto.lg.jp/detail.php?InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000415 Kyoto Tourism Navi - Hōgon-ji (Ushio Kannon, Ushioyama)]. This belief laid the foundation for the mountain worship of Hieyama (Mount Hiei). Additionally, the *Kojiki* mentions that [[Oyamakui no Kami]], also called Yamasue-no-Ōnushi-no-Kami, dwells on Mount Hiei (日枝山) and at {{ill|Matsuo Taisha|ja|松尾大社|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Kadono District|ja|葛野郡|12=draft|12=draft}}. Furthermore, from {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, where {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主|12=draft|12=draft}} is worshipped as the *nigi-mitama* (gentle spirit) of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴神|12=draft|12=draft}}, the deity was enshrined at Mount Hiei. This process established {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft|12=draft}}, making it one of the most syncretic shrines, serving as the guardian deity of the Tendai school and its temple complex[https://www.asahi.com/travel/hikyou/TKY201103270195.html "Japan's Tradition: Shinbutsu-shūgō (2)" - Asahi Shimbun Digital, March 28, 2011]. {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft|12=draft}} maintained a close relationship, with Enryaku-ji holding authority over appointments within Hiyoshi Taisha from the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft|12=draft}} onward. As Tendai Buddhism spread throughout Japan, temples dedicated to Sannō Gongen, called '''Sannō-sha''', were established across the country. In addition to Sannō Gongen, Tendai Buddhism also enshrined {{ill|Hachioji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen|12=draft|12=draft}} at Mount Hiei and assigned {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹|12=draft|12=draft}} Buddhas to the 21 Sannō Shrines. At the beginning of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the shrine priests of Hiyoshi Taisha attempted to abolish Shinbutsu-shūgō and establish a form of exclusive Shinto known as {{ill|Yoshida Shintō|ja|唯一神道|Yoshida Shintō=Yoshida Shintō|lt=Ikkō Shintō|12=draft|12=draft}}. This led them to burn Buddhist images and engage in an early attempt at {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|12=draft|12=draft}}, the movement to eliminate Buddhism. However, Enryaku-ji appealed to the shogunate, resulting in a court ruling against Hiyoshi Taisha. The shrine priests were exiled, and the shrine was placed under complete control of Enryaku-ji. During this period, Enryaku-ji established a rule that "any actions beyond festivals and cleaning must receive permission from Enryaku-ji". From that point on, Hiyoshi Taisha was also financially dependent on Enryaku-ji. == Separation of Kami and Buddhas / Haibutsu Kishaku == During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft|12=draft}}, the government implemented {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft|12=draft}}, leading to the abolition of Sannō Gongen, the guardian deity of Tendai Buddhism. Hiyoshi Taisha was one of the first sites to undergo the {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離令|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Keiō|ja|慶応|12=draft|12=draft}} 4 (1868). Hiyoshi Sannō Shrine was forcibly separated from Enryaku-jiBecause the Shinto faction engaged in aggressive destruction of Buddhist-related objects, the new government issued a directive on April 10, Keiō 4, to implement Shinbutsu Bunri more peacefully. and reorganized into {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft|12=draft}}. When the shrine attempted to take custody of its buildings' keys from Enryaku-ji, they were refused. In response, the shrine administrator, {{ill|Kiju Shigekuni|ja|樹下茂国|12=draft|12=draft}}, along with the Shinto guards and 100 hired farmers, stormed the shrine and destroyed 124 Buddhist statues, artifacts, and scriptures, while confiscating 48 bronze objects, including temple gongs. The seized metal was repurposed for cannon and currency production. This incident set a precedent for Haibutsu Kishaku across Japan. Today, many remaining {{ill|Sannō-sha|ja|山王社|12=draft|12=draft}} shrines have become {{ill|Hie Shrines|ja|日枝神社|12=draft|12=draft}} or {{ill|Hiyoshi Shrines|ja|日吉神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, enshrining {{ill|Ōyamakui-no-Kami|ja|大山咋神|12=draft|12=draft}} as their principal deity. == Hie Sannō Mandala (日吉山王曼荼羅図) == '''Hie Sannō Mandala''' (日吉山王曼荼羅図) is a mandala depicting the Buddhas and deities enshrined at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|12=draft|12=draft}}, composed according to the doctrines of {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft|12=draft}} Buddhism. Based on its format, it can be classified as a {{ill|Miya Mandala|ja|宮曼荼羅|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Honjibutsu Mandala|ja|本地仏曼荼羅|12=draft|12=draft}}, or {{ill|Suijaku Mandala|ja|垂迹神曼荼羅|12=draft|12=draft}}. === Miya Mandala (宮曼荼羅図) === [[File:Sanno Miya Mandala.jpg|thumb|right|150px|Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala (Muromachi period)
The upper section displays the twenty-one shrine deities, their honjibutsu (Buddhist counterparts), and their {{ill|seed syllables|ja|種子 (密教)|12=draft|12=draft}}. The lower section provides a bird's-eye view of the twenty-one shrines, depicting two shrines at the summit of {{ill|Mount Hachioji|ja|八王子山|12=draft|12=draft}} and the shrine complex at the foot of the mountain.]] The Miya Mandala is a depiction of shrine landscapes from an aerial perspective. These mandalas were created so that individuals unable to visit the shrine in person could worship from home as a substitute for pilgrimage. Examples designated as Important Cultural Properties include "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala" (held by the {{ill|Yamato Bunkakan|ja|大和文華館|12=draft|12=draft}}, Nara City) and "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala" (held by {{ill|Hyakusaiji Temple|ja|百済寺|12=draft|12=draft}}, Higashiomi City), as well as "Silk-colored Sannō Hōtō Mandala" (held by the [[:en:Ōtani University|Ōtani University Museum]], Kyoto City). === Honjibutsu Mandala (本地仏曼荼羅図) === The Honjibutsu Mandala illustrates the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=draft|12=draft}} in their original Buddhist forms, depicting them seated as regular Buddhist statues within shrine buildings. === Suijaku Mandala (垂迹神曼荼羅図) === Contrasting with the Honjibutsu Mandala, the Suijaku Mandala depicts the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=draft|12=draft}} in their Shinto god forms. It portrays a scene where the deities of Mount Hiei gather in a single shrine. Examples designated as Important Cultural Properties include the "Hie Sannō Suijaku Mandala" (held by {{ill|Saikyōji Temple|ja|西教寺|12=draft|12=draft}}, Otsu City), as well as "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Suijaku Mandala" (held by {{ill|Sannō Lecture|ja|山王講|12=draft|12=draft}}, Kurano-tsuji Tomokura) and "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Jūzenji Mandala" (held by {{ill|Shinnyo-en|ja|真如苑|12=draft|12=draft}}, Tokyo). == Honjibutsu of the 21 Sannō Shrines (山王21社の本地仏) == {| class="wikitable" ! !! Former Name !! Honjibutsu !! Location |- |rowspan=7|Upper Seven Shrines

[[File:Mount Hiei from Kyoto - IMG 5701.JPG|thumb|right|300px|Mount Hiei]] '''Sannō Gongen''' (山王権現) is a {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|12=draft|12=draft}} deity that emerged from the fusion of {{ill|mountain worship|ja|山岳信仰|12=draft|12=draft}} on Mount Hiei ({{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|12=draft|12=draft}}), {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft|12=draft}} Buddhism. It serves as the {{ill|chinjugami|ja|鎮守神|lt=guardian deity|12=draft|12=draft}} of the Tendai school. It was also referred to as '''Hiyoshi Gongen''' or '''Hiyoshi Sannō Gongen'''. == History == Sannō Gongen is the {{ill|chinjugami|ja|鎮守神|lt=guardian deity|12=draft|12=draft}} of {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft|12=draft}}, formed through the fusion of {{ill|mountain worship|ja|山岳信仰|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft|12=draft}} on Mount Hiei ({{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|12=draft|12=draft}}). It is also sometimes used to refer to the {{ill|saijin|ja|祭神|lt=deities enshrined|12=draft|12=draft}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft|12=draft}}. Sannō Gongen, as the deity of Mount Hiei, is also affectionately called "Hiyossan" (Hiyoshi-san) and is enshrined in all [[:Category:Hiyoshi Shrines|Hiyoshi Shrines]] nationwide, with {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft|12=draft}} serving as the head shrine[http://www.rekihaku.otsu.shiga.jp/news/1104.html "Special Exhibition on Kami and Buddhas in Ōmi" (Ōtsu City Museum of History, September 3, 2011)]. The term "Hiyoshi Sannō" refers to the blended faith that regarded Mount Hiei as a "sacred mountain," integrating the belief systems of both {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft|12=draft}}. At {{ill|Guoqing Temple|ja|国清寺|12=draft|12=draft}} on {{ill|Mount Tiantai|ja|天台山|12=draft|12=draft}}, where {{ill|Saichō|ja|最澄|12=draft|12=draft}} (Dengyō Daishi), the founder of Japanese Tendai Buddhism, studied, the guardian deity was '''Jizhu Sannō Yuanbi Zhenjun''' (地主山王元弼真君), a deified prince of {{ill|King Ling of Zhou|ja|霊王_(周)|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Daoism|ja|道教|12=draft|12=draft}}. When Saichō returned to Japan, he enshrined Sannō Gongen as the guardian deity of {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft|12=draft}}, modeling it after Guoqing Temple. The peak of {{ill|Otowa Mountain|ja|音羽山_(滋賀県・京都府)|12=draft|12=draft}}, known as Ushioyama in ancient times, was revered as a sacred place where households offered their first harvest to the gods[http://kanko.city.kyoto.lg.jp/detail.php?InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000415 Kyoto Tourism Navi - Hōgon-ji (Ushio Kannon, Ushioyama)]. This belief laid the foundation for the mountain worship of Hieyama (Mount Hiei). Additionally, the *Kojiki* mentions that [[Oyamakui no Kami]], also called Yamasue-no-Ōnushi-no-Kami, dwells on Mount Hiei (日枝山) and at {{ill|Matsuo Taisha|ja|松尾大社|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Kadono District|ja|葛野郡|12=draft|12=draft}}. Furthermore, from {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, where {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主|12=draft|12=draft}} is worshipped as the *nigi-mitama* (gentle spirit) of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴神|12=draft|12=draft}}, the deity was enshrined at Mount Hiei. This process established {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft|12=draft}}, making it one of the most syncretic shrines, serving as the guardian deity of the Tendai school and its temple complex[https://www.asahi.com/travel/hikyou/TKY201103270195.html "Japan's Tradition: Shinbutsu-shūgō (2)" - Asahi Shimbun Digital, March 28, 2011]. {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft|12=draft}} maintained a close relationship, with Enryaku-ji holding authority over appointments within Hiyoshi Taisha from the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft|12=draft}} onward. As Tendai Buddhism spread throughout Japan, temples dedicated to Sannō Gongen, called '''Sannō-sha''', were established across the country. In addition to Sannō Gongen, Tendai Buddhism also enshrined {{ill|Hachioji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen|12=draft|12=draft}} at Mount Hiei and assigned {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹|12=draft|12=draft}} Buddhas to the 21 Sannō Shrines. At the beginning of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the shrine priests of Hiyoshi Taisha attempted to abolish Shinbutsu-shūgō and establish a form of exclusive Shinto known as {{ill|Yoshida Shintō|ja|唯一神道|Yoshida Shintō=Yoshida Shintō|lt=Ikkō Shintō|12=draft|12=draft}}. This led them to burn Buddhist images and engage in an early attempt at {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|1=Haibutsu kishaku|12=draft|12=draft}}, the movement to eliminate Buddhism. However, Enryaku-ji appealed to the shogunate, resulting in a court ruling against Hiyoshi Taisha. The shrine priests were exiled, and the shrine was placed under complete control of Enryaku-ji. During this period, Enryaku-ji established a rule that "any actions beyond festivals and cleaning must receive permission from Enryaku-ji". From that point on, Hiyoshi Taisha was also financially dependent on Enryaku-ji. == Separation of Kami and Buddhas / Haibutsu Kishaku == During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft|12=draft}}, the government implemented {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft|12=draft}}, leading to the abolition of Sannō Gongen, the guardian deity of Tendai Buddhism. Hiyoshi Taisha was one of the first sites to undergo the {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離令|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Keiō|ja|慶応|12=draft|12=draft}} 4 (1868). Hiyoshi Sannō Shrine was forcibly separated from Enryaku-jiBecause the Shinto faction engaged in aggressive destruction of Buddhist-related objects, the new government issued a directive on April 10, Keiō 4, to implement Shinbutsu Bunri more peacefully. and reorganized into {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft|12=draft}}. When the shrine attempted to take custody of its buildings' keys from Enryaku-ji, they were refused. In response, the shrine administrator, {{ill|Kiju Shigekuni|ja|樹下茂国|12=draft|12=draft}}, along with the Shinto guards and 100 hired farmers, stormed the shrine and destroyed 124 Buddhist statues, artifacts, and scriptures, while confiscating 48 bronze objects, including temple gongs. The seized metal was repurposed for cannon and currency production. This incident set a precedent for Haibutsu Kishaku across Japan. Today, many remaining {{ill|Sannō-sha|ja|山王社|12=draft|12=draft}} shrines have become {{ill|Hie Shrines|ja|日枝神社|12=draft|12=draft}} or {{ill|Hiyoshi Shrines|ja|日吉神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, enshrining {{ill|Ōyamakui-no-Kami|ja|大山咋神|12=draft|12=draft}} as their principal deity. == Hie Sannō Mandala (日吉山王曼荼羅図) == '''Hie Sannō Mandala''' (日吉山王曼荼羅図) is a mandala depicting the Buddhas and deities enshrined at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|12=draft|12=draft}}, composed according to the doctrines of {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft|12=draft}} Buddhism. Based on its format, it can be classified as a {{ill|Miya Mandala|ja|宮曼荼羅|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Honjibutsu Mandala|ja|本地仏曼荼羅|12=draft|12=draft}}, or {{ill|Suijaku Mandala|ja|垂迹神曼荼羅|12=draft|12=draft}}. === Miya Mandala (宮曼荼羅図) === [[File:Sanno Miya Mandala.jpg|thumb|right|150px|Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala (Muromachi period)
The upper section displays the twenty-one shrine deities, their honjibutsu (Buddhist counterparts), and their {{ill|seed syllables|ja|種子 (密教)|12=draft|12=draft}}. The lower section provides a bird's-eye view of the twenty-one shrines, depicting two shrines at the summit of {{ill|Mount Hachioji|ja|八王子山|12=draft|12=draft}} and the shrine complex at the foot of the mountain.]] The Miya Mandala is a depiction of shrine landscapes from an aerial perspective. These mandalas were created so that individuals unable to visit the shrine in person could worship from home as a substitute for pilgrimage. Examples designated as Important Cultural Properties include "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala" (held by the {{ill|Yamato Bunkakan|ja|大和文華館|12=draft|12=draft}}, Nara City) and "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala" (held by {{ill|Hyakusaiji Temple|ja|百済寺|12=draft|12=draft}}, Higashiomi City), as well as "Silk-colored Sannō Hōtō Mandala" (held by the [[:en:Ōtani University|Ōtani University Museum]], Kyoto City). === Honjibutsu Mandala (本地仏曼荼羅図) === The Honjibutsu Mandala illustrates the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=draft|12=draft}} in their original Buddhist forms, depicting them seated as regular Buddhist statues within shrine buildings. === Suijaku Mandala (垂迹神曼荼羅図) === Contrasting with the Honjibutsu Mandala, the Suijaku Mandala depicts the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=draft|12=draft}} in their Shinto god forms. It portrays a scene where the deities of Mount Hiei gather in a single shrine. Examples designated as Important Cultural Properties include the "Hie Sannō Suijaku Mandala" (held by {{ill|Saikyōji Temple|ja|西教寺|12=draft|12=draft}}, Otsu City), as well as "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Suijaku Mandala" (held by {{ill|Sannō Lecture|ja|山王講|12=draft|12=draft}}, Kurano-tsuji Tomokura) and "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Jūzenji Mandala" (held by {{ill|Shinnyo-en|ja|真如苑|12=draft|12=draft}}, Tokyo). == Honjibutsu of the 21 Sannō Shrines (山王21社の本地仏) == {| class="wikitable" ! !! revision.text:
[[File:Mount Hiei from Kyoto - IMG 5701.JPG|thumb|right|300px|Mount Hiei]] '''Sannō Gongen''' (山王権現) is a {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|12=draft|12=draft}} deity that emerged from the fusion of {{ill|mountain worship|ja|山岳信仰|12=draft|12=draft}} on Mount Hiei ({{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|12=draft|12=draft}}), {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft|12=draft}} Buddhism. It serves as the {{ill|chinjugami|ja|鎮守神|lt=guardian deity|12=draft|12=draft}} of the Tendai school. It was also referred to as '''Hiyoshi Gongen''' or '''Hiyoshi Sannō Gongen'''. == History == Sannō Gongen is the {{ill|chinjugami|ja|鎮守神|lt=guardian deity|12=draft|12=draft}} of {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft|12=draft}}, formed through the fusion of {{ill|mountain worship|ja|山岳信仰|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft|12=draft}} on Mount Hiei ({{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|12=draft|12=draft}}). It is also sometimes used to refer to the {{ill|saijin|ja|祭神|lt=deities enshrined|12=draft|12=draft}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft|12=draft}}. Sannō Gongen, as the deity of Mount Hiei, is also affectionately called "Hiyossan" (Hiyoshi-san) and is enshrined in all [[:Category:Hiyoshi Shrines|Hiyoshi Shrines]] nationwide, with {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft|12=draft}} serving as the head shrine[http://www.rekihaku.otsu.shiga.jp/news/1104.html "Special Exhibition on Kami and Buddhas in Ōmi" (Ōtsu City Museum of History, September 3, 2011)]. The term "Hiyoshi Sannō" refers to the blended faith that regarded Mount Hiei as a "sacred mountain," integrating the belief systems of both {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft|12=draft}}. At {{ill|Guoqing Temple|ja|国清寺|12=draft|12=draft}} on {{ill|Mount Tiantai|ja|天台山|12=draft|12=draft}}, where {{ill|Saichō|ja|最澄|12=draft|12=draft}} (Dengyō Daishi), the founder of Japanese Tendai Buddhism, studied, the guardian deity was '''Jizhu Sannō Yuanbi Zhenjun''' (地主山王元弼真君), a deified prince of {{ill|King Ling of Zhou|ja|霊王_(周)|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Daoism|ja|道教|12=draft|12=draft}}. When Saichō returned to Japan, he enshrined Sannō Gongen as the guardian deity of {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft|12=draft}}, modeling it after Guoqing Temple. The peak of {{ill|Otowa Mountain|ja|音羽山_(滋賀県・京都府)|12=draft|12=draft}}, known as Ushioyama in ancient times, was revered as a sacred place where households offered their first harvest to the gods[http://kanko.city.kyoto.lg.jp/detail.php?InforKindCode=1&ManageCode=1000415 Kyoto Tourism Navi - Hōgon-ji (Ushio Kannon, Ushioyama)]. This belief laid the foundation for the mountain worship of Hieyama (Mount Hiei). Additionally, the *Kojiki* mentions that [[Oyamakui no Kami]], also called Yamasue-no-Ōnushi-no-Kami, dwells on Mount Hiei (日枝山) and at {{ill|Matsuo Taisha|ja|松尾大社|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Kadono District|ja|葛野郡|12=draft|12=draft}}. Furthermore, from {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, where {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主|12=draft|12=draft}} is worshipped as the *nigi-mitama* (gentle spirit) of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴神|12=draft|12=draft}}, the deity was enshrined at Mount Hiei. This process established {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft|12=draft}}, making it one of the most syncretic shrines, serving as the guardian deity of the Tendai school and its temple complex[https://www.asahi.com/travel/hikyou/TKY201103270195.html "Japan's Tradition: Shinbutsu-shūgō (2)" - Asahi Shimbun Digital, March 28, 2011]. {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft|12=draft}} maintained a close relationship, with Enryaku-ji holding authority over appointments within Hiyoshi Taisha from the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft|12=draft}} onward. As Tendai Buddhism spread throughout Japan, temples dedicated to Sannō Gongen, called '''Sannō-sha''', were established across the country. In addition to Sannō Gongen, Tendai Buddhism also enshrined {{ill|Hachioji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen|12=draft|12=draft}} at Mount Hiei and assigned {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹|12=draft|12=draft}} Buddhas to the 21 Sannō Shrines. At the beginning of the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the shrine priests of Hiyoshi Taisha attempted to abolish Shinbutsu-shūgō and establish a form of exclusive Shinto known as {{ill|Yoshida Shintō|ja|唯一神道|Yoshida Shintō=Yoshida Shintō|lt=Ikkō Shintō|12=draft|12=draft}}. This led them to burn Buddhist images and engage in an early attempt at {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|1=Haibutsu kishaku|12=draft|12=draft}}, the movement to eliminate Buddhism. However, Enryaku-ji appealed to the shogunate, resulting in a court ruling against Hiyoshi Taisha. The shrine priests were exiled, and the shrine was placed under complete control of Enryaku-ji. During this period, Enryaku-ji established a rule that "any actions beyond festivals and cleaning must receive permission from Enryaku-ji". From that point on, Hiyoshi Taisha was also financially dependent on Enryaku-ji. == Separation of Kami and Buddhas / Haibutsu Kishaku == During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft|12=draft}}, the government implemented {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft|12=draft}}, leading to the abolition of Sannō Gongen, the guardian deity of Tendai Buddhism. Hiyoshi Taisha was one of the first sites to undergo the {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離令|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Keiō|ja|慶応|12=draft|12=draft}} 4 (1868). Hiyoshi Sannō Shrine was forcibly separated from Enryaku-jiBecause the Shinto faction engaged in aggressive destruction of Buddhist-related objects, the new government issued a directive on April 10, Keiō 4, to implement Shinbutsu Bunri more peacefully. and reorganized into {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft|12=draft}}. When the shrine attempted to take custody of its buildings' keys from Enryaku-ji, they were refused. In response, the shrine administrator, {{ill|Kiju Shigekuni|ja|樹下茂国|12=draft|12=draft}}, along with the Shinto guards and 100 hired farmers, stormed the shrine and destroyed 124 Buddhist statues, artifacts, and scriptures, while confiscating 48 bronze objects, including temple gongs. The seized metal was repurposed for cannon and currency production. This incident set a precedent for Haibutsu Kishaku across Japan. Today, many remaining {{ill|Sannō-sha|ja|山王社|12=draft|12=draft}} shrines have become {{ill|Hie Shrines|ja|日枝神社|12=draft|12=draft}} or {{ill|Hiyoshi Shrines|ja|日吉神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, enshrining {{ill|Ōyamakui-no-Kami|ja|大山咋神|12=draft|12=draft}} as their principal deity. == Hie Sannō Mandala (日吉山王曼荼羅図) == '''Hie Sannō Mandala''' (日吉山王曼荼羅図) is a mandala depicting the Buddhas and deities enshrined at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|12=draft|12=draft}}, composed according to the doctrines of {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft|12=draft}} Buddhism. Based on its format, it can be classified as a {{ill|Miya Mandala|ja|宮曼荼羅|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Honjibutsu Mandala|ja|本地仏曼荼羅|12=draft|12=draft}}, or {{ill|Suijaku Mandala|ja|垂迹神曼荼羅|12=draft|12=draft}}. === Miya Mandala (宮曼荼羅図) === [[File:Sanno Miya Mandala.jpg|thumb|right|150px|Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala (Muromachi period)
The upper section displays the twenty-one shrine deities, their honjibutsu (Buddhist counterparts), and their {{ill|seed syllables|ja|種子 (密教)|12=draft|12=draft}}. The lower section provides a bird's-eye view of the twenty-one shrines, depicting two shrines at the summit of {{ill|Mount Hachioji|ja|八王子山|12=draft|12=draft}} and the shrine complex at the foot of the mountain.]] The Miya Mandala is a depiction of shrine landscapes from an aerial perspective. These mandalas were created so that individuals unable to visit the shrine in person could worship from home as a substitute for pilgrimage. Examples designated as Important Cultural Properties include "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala" (held by the {{ill|Yamato Bunkakan|ja|大和文華館|12=draft|12=draft}}, Nara City) and "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Miya Mandala" (held by {{ill|Hyakusaiji Temple|ja|百済寺|12=draft|12=draft}}, Higashiomi City), as well as "Silk-colored Sannō Hōtō Mandala" (held by the [[:en:Ōtani University|Ōtani University Museum]], Kyoto City). === Honjibutsu Mandala (本地仏曼荼羅図) === The Honjibutsu Mandala illustrates the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=draft|12=draft}} in their original Buddhist forms, depicting them seated as regular Buddhist statues within shrine buildings. === Suijaku Mandala (垂迹神曼荼羅図) === Contrasting with the Honjibutsu Mandala, the Suijaku Mandala depicts the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=draft|12=draft}} in their Shinto god forms. It portrays a scene where the deities of Mount Hiei gather in a single shrine. Examples designated as Important Cultural Properties include the "Hie Sannō Suijaku Mandala" (held by {{ill|Saikyōji Temple|ja|西教寺|12=draft|12=draft}}, Otsu City), as well as "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Suijaku Mandala" (held by {{ill|Sannō Lecture|ja|山王講|12=draft|12=draft}}, Kurano-tsuji Tomokura) and "Silk-colored Hie Sannō Jūzenji Mandala" (held by {{ill|Shinnyo-en|ja|真如苑|12=draft|12=draft}}, Tokyo). == Honjibutsu of the 21 Sannō Shrines (山王21社の本地仏) == {| class="wikitable" ! !!
